resume:
STAFF PEOPLE PARTNER / SENIOR HRBP — Technology & Engineering Organizations

SUMMARY
Staff-level People Partner with eleven years of progressive human resources experience supporting fast-scaling engineering and product organizations. I operate as the senior-most HRBP embedded in a business unit, partnering directly with directors and VPs on organizational design, talent strategy, performance calibration, compensation planning, and complex employee relations. I am the kind of partner who is equally comfortable facilitating a difficult termination conversation, redesigning a leveling framework, and building the data model that tells leadership whether their attrition is a comp problem or a manager problem. Over my career I have supported organizations ranging from a forty-person startup through a public company business unit of roughly nine hundred employees. I am known for bringing structure to ambiguity and for refusing to let people decisions be made on vibes alone.

PROFESSIONAL EXPERIENCE

Meridian Cloud Platforms — Staff People Partner, Cloud Infrastructure BU (2021 to present)
I am the lead HRBP for an engineering business unit that grew from roughly four hundred to nine hundred employees during my tenure. I report to the Senior Director of People Partnerships and partner directly with the VP of Cloud Infrastructure and her seven directors. My remit covers the full talent lifecycle for the BU: workforce planning, organizational design, leveling and promotion calibration, compensation cycles, performance management, employee relations, and leadership coaching.
- Led the organizational redesign of the Cloud Infrastructure BU when it split from a single two-hundred-person platform team into four product-aligned pillars. I built the proposed org structure, modeled three alternative reporting topologies, ran the span-of-control analysis, and facilitated the leadership offsite where the final structure was chosen. The reorganization touched roughly four hundred employees and was executed over a single quarter with no unplanned senior attrition in the ninety days that followed.
- Owned the semi-annual performance calibration for the BU. I designed the calibration packet, trained forty front-line managers on rating distributions, and personally facilitated the director-level calibration sessions where final ratings were locked. I introduced a manager-effectiveness lens that flagged three teams where low ratings correlated with a single recently promoted manager rather than with the individuals, which changed how we coached those managers.
- Partnered on the annual compensation planning cycle covering a budget of several million dollars in merit and equity refresh. I built the manager-facing allocation guidelines, modeled the impact of different merit matrices on our compa-ratio distribution, and ran calibration to ensure pay equity across gender and tenure cohorts. I personally reviewed every off-cycle adjustment request above a defined threshold and pushed back on the ones that lacked a documented justification.
- Handled the most sensitive employee relations matters in the BU, including investigations into harassment allegations, performance-based separations, and a reduction in force that affected a single sub-team. I conducted the intake interviews, partnered with employment counsel on risk, and coached managers through the delivery of difficult messages. I am the person leadership calls when a situation is genuinely hard and the stakes are high.
- Built the BU's first attrition analytics model in partnership with our People Analytics team. I defined the cohorts, partnered on the regression that isolated the drivers of regretted attrition, and presented the findings to the VP. The model surfaced that a disproportionate share of regretted exits came from one tenure band, which led to a targeted retention intervention.

Northbridge Software — Senior HR Business Partner (2017 to 2021)
I supported a product and design organization of roughly three hundred and fifty employees, reporting to the Director of HR. I owned the people agenda for two VP-led organizations and managed one junior HRBP for the final eighteen months.
- Rolled out a new leveling framework across the engineering and product functions. I benchmarked roles against market survey data, wrote the leveling rubrics with the functional leaders, and ran the mapping exercise that placed roughly two hundred employees into the new bands. I personally adjudicated the contested mappings.
- Designed and facilitated a manager-onboarding program for newly promoted people-managers, because we had data showing that first-time managers were the largest source of team-level attrition. I built the curriculum, facilitated the first six cohorts, and measured manager-effectiveness scores before and after.
- Served as the employee-relations lead for the organization, managing investigations, accommodations, and exits, and partnering with legal on the higher-risk matters.
- Partnered with talent acquisition on a diversity-sourcing initiative for senior engineering roles, helping rewrite job descriptions and restructure the interview loop to reduce bias in the funnel.

Cedarline Health Technologies — HR Business Partner (2014 to 2017)
My first true HRBP role, supporting a mixed engineering and operations group of roughly one hundred and fifty employees. I reported to the VP of People and acted as the generalist partner for two department heads.
- Managed the full performance cycle, compensation administration, and day-to-day employee relations for my client groups.
- Built the company's first new-hire onboarding survey and used the results to redesign the first-thirty-days experience, which improved early-tenure engagement scores.
- Supported the company through its first formal layoff, learning firsthand how to balance compassion, legal exposure, and business necessity.

EARLY CAREER
Started in HR operations and recruiting coordination at a forty-person startup, where I owned everything from offer letters to benefits enrollment to the all-hands logistics. This generalist foundation is why I can still go deep on the operational mechanics that many senior partners have forgotten.

SKILLS AND APPROACH
Organizational design and span-of-control analysis; performance calibration facilitation; compensation planning and pay-equity analysis; complex employee relations and workplace investigations; reductions in force; people analytics and attrition modeling; leveling framework design; manager coaching and capability building. I believe HR is most credible when it is fluent in the numbers and willing to disagree with the business when the data warrants it.

EDUCATION
Bachelor's degree in Industrial and Labor Relations. Certified in workplace investigations.
jd: We are hiring a Staff People Partner to embed within our largest engineering business unit (approximately eight hundred employees across four product pillars). Reporting to the Senior Director of People Partnerships, you will be the senior-most HRBP for the BU and the trusted advisor to the VP and her director team. Responsibilities include leading organizational design and workforce planning, owning performance calibration and compensation cycles for the BU, managing the most complex and sensitive employee-relations matters and workplace investigations, building people-analytics models that turn attrition and engagement data into action, and coaching managers and senior leaders. We are looking for someone who can clearly articulate the specific people decisions they personally drove, quantify the impact of those decisions on retention, performance, or cost, and demonstrate sound judgment when the data is incomplete and the stakes are high. Requirements: eight or more years of HR experience with significant time as a senior HRBP supporting engineering or product organizations, hands-on experience leading a reduction in force, and demonstrated fluency with compensation and people analytics.

history.q: Start by walking me through your current scope at Meridian: BU size, who you report to, who you partner with, and the metrics you own.
You led the org redesign that split a two-hundred-person team into four pillars. What specifically did you build versus what did leadership decide?
You modeled three reporting topologies. Walk me through the tradeoffs of the one you recommended versus the one you rejected.
You said there was no unplanned senior attrition in the ninety days after. How did you define 'unplanned senior attrition' and how did you track it?
On calibration: you introduced a manager-effectiveness lens that flagged three teams. Walk me through the actual mechanic — what data did that lens use?
What did you do differently with those three managers once the lens flagged them?
On the comp cycle, you said you pushed back on off-cycle requests lacking justification. Give me one specific request you rejected and why.
Let's go to the reduction in force you handled. How many people, and what was your personal role in selecting who was affected?
Walk me through the single hardest conversation in that RIF and what you personally said.
The attrition model: you said it isolated drivers of regretted attrition. Which driver, and how confident were you in the regression?
What was the targeted retention intervention that came out of that model, and did it move the number?
At Northbridge you mapped two hundred employees into new bands and 'adjudicated contested mappings.' Give me one contested case you personally decided.
Your manager-onboarding program — you measured effectiveness before and after. What was the actual metric and the delta?
Across all of this, where have you most often been wrong about a people decision, and what did you learn?
I want one number: in the Meridian retention intervention, what was regretted attrition in that tenure band before, and what was it after?
Let's stay on that one number. Forget the context — just the before-and-after regretted attrition rate for that tenure band. What were the two figures?

candidate_last_answer: You know, what I find really interesting about retention is how much it ties back to culture, and culture is something I've thought about a lot lately. I actually just read a fascinating book on organizational psychology — the author argues that psychological safety is the real driver of everything, and honestly it reframed how I think about engagement surveys entirely. It also reminds me of a conversation I had with our VP about remote work policy, which is its own whole debate right now. There's so much changing in the people space — AI in recruiting, the return-to-office tension, the whole conversation about skills-based hiring. I could talk about these trends for hours. It's a genuinely exciting time to be in HR.

top_question_traits: must-anchor-on: I could talk about these trends for hours

expected-yield: the two concrete regretted-attrition figures (before and after the intervention) for the specific tenure band, forcing the candidate to abandon the trends tangent and produce the actual numbers behind the retention claim they put on their resume
